story:
Once upon a childhood, Jimmy wandered off at a wildlife sanctuary while Dad joked he was only taking his wildlife to visit other wildlife. Jimmy giggled. Tiarna, his big sister, rolled her eyes and told him not to be annoying, as usual. It was a blazing, scorching day. Tiarna was glued to her phone. Jimmy, nine and prickly, drifted down a skinny forest track, thirsty and grumpy because he’d left his water bottle in the car again.

He slumped by a rockery, insects buzzing in his ears, and heard, very distinctly, “Who are you?” A tiny brown lizard blinked at him, sunbaking like a boss. “I’m the Great Australian skink,” it said. Jimmy cupped his hands, scooped it up, and slipped it into his pocket. Hah, he thought, a new pet. Not a great plan.

He sauntered on, ready to ask his talking lizard how to get back to the kiosk, when the pocket wriggled. The skink shot free—minus its tail—scuttling for the rocks. “Detachable tail!” it called. “Have a great day!” Jimmy glared. “Don’t tell me what to do!” he snapped, then eyed the tail and thought—briefly and badly—about collecting more for nature studies. The pale, twitchy thing made his stomach flip. He stuffed it deeper, already not liking it.

He kept to the narrow track and came upon emus, a whole family pecking seeds from the dirt. He decided a feather would be cool and crept toward a chick. As he tugged, the chick spun, and its parents did too. Beaks open. Eyes lit up with don’t-even-think-about-it. Jimmy bolted. For a nine-year-old, he was fast, but the emus were faster. Their long legs pounded after him, beaks snapping, the once-sleepy sanctuary roaring awake. A hot wind rose. The big trees thrashed. Clouds stacked into a wall. Prickles clawed his arms and legs, but he kept running, stealing glances back at two furious faces that felt as if they burned red.

There—buildings. He threw everything into a final sprint and made the kiosk steps just ahead of those stomping legs. The emus skidded away down the ramp and vanished into the slashing rain as lightning stitched the sky. He burst inside and stopped. Empty. No families, no staff, no annoying big sister. The lights hummed, the fridges glowed, and there were cold drinks sitting right there. He cracked one, figuring Dad would sort the bill like always. Better that than collapse. He touched his pocket: the skink’s tail. It felt gross now. Maybe he’d put it back outside when the storm eased.

Then the power died. The kiosk went dim, like sudden night. A sign by the propped-open door flapped at him: If you are reading this sign, and it blows away, there is a cyclone! Run to the Evacuation Centre under the reptile house. Thunder slammed the roof. A gust tore the sign off its hook and it blew away. Running into flying branches and sheets of water felt worse than staying, so he kicked the door shut, crawled under a big table with a few cakes grabbed on the way, and wished hard that Mum, Dad—and yes, even Tiarna—were safe. Mean thoughts fizzled. He didn’t want anything bad to happen to anyone.

The cyclone raged, then moved on. The roof held. The forest breathed again. Jimmy dozed among empty wrappers, dreaming the Great Australian skink laughed at him—fair enough. When he woke, the door, stuck with damp, wouldn’t budge. Rangers shouldered it open. Mum and Dad rushed in, hugging him until he squeaked. He hadn’t been blown away. He scribbled an IOU for the drinks and snacks; Dad would settle up later since the power lines were down. Outside, the wildlife crept from their hideaways. Jimmy tucked the skink’s cast-off tail where scavengers could find it and decided he wouldn’t be collecting anything that still belonged to something alive. He also decided he’d stick with his family, keep his hands off feathers, and, next time, follow the signs.

Overall, the family trip to visit other wildlife was, in every sense, cyclonic.
